data: Experimental 3D game as the semester project for BA4, which involved a custom hardware external controller housed in a walk-in box, controlling a 3D Mech model running in the Unity client. I focused implementing the Arduino code, bridging it with Unity through serial communication, and the UI Toolkit based menu.

Mathematics-based card fighting game developed for a client as part of the BA6 semester project, which focused on fractions. For the project, I developed a custom fraction based type system in C#, along with implementing card interactions in Unity.
My second Portfolio website, created with React and Framer-Motion. By this time, I had acquired some more skills, that I could list them in a section.
My third and current Portfolio website, generated from a Statamic backend through a SvelteKit frontend. The unique mechanism of generating the website is something inspired by my work at Uni-Bonn.

During my work at Uni Bonn, I helped port a vanilla KoaJS backend to a headless Strapi CMS instance. That gave me the idea to create the next iteration of my portfolio website, dubbed Portfolio V3, in the same way. My previous two portfolio websites used Angular and React, with all the content manually written in. As a small website with little content, it makes sense to just generate a static website and host it somewhere, such as Github Pages. However, this also meant adding content manually as well. What if instead I stored all my content in a CMS, loaded them in the frontend, and then ran the static site generation? Thus came about this portfolio website, which also includes this simple case study that you're reading.

Since I am also taking this opportunity to learn something new while I am at it, I opted for Statamic, a CMS based on Laravel and PHP. Statamic provides its own frontend mechanisms, along with static site generation, however it can also be run headless through REST as well as GraphQL. In order to make data fetching easier, I used urql, a library that queries Statamic's GraphQL API. I can easily write up the query in a single object, which includes the title, content, and related media, without having to worry about parameters in a typical REST API call. Although it requires a Pro license in order to run a live site with the REST and GraphQL APIs enabled, since I am not hosting Statamic itself, I can just run the frontend static site generator and host the built site. Statamic Pro has an infinite trial as long as it's during development, so I can get the benefits for my unique use case.

Since the previous websites used Angular and React, and I already use Vue at work, this time I opted for a Svelte frontend. More specifically, SvelteKit, which comes with its own file based route mechanism. Svelte separates routes into files and folders, where each folder is a route. Every route has a +page.svelte file for rendering, and +layout.svelte file for common components to be rendered in multiple routes. For client side JavaScript (or TypeScript), a +page.ts file can be included, and +page.server.ts for server side functionality. Using the adapter for static site generation, the functions I put in +page.server.ts will load before being rendered statically.

To style the website, I used BulmaCSS. Of course, using a TailwindCSS library might have made it a lot faster, since that's what I am used to, but I wanted to see how Bulma also works, and it even comes with a small component library of its own, which covers the use case for a portfolio website like mine.

With all that out of the way, the hardest part is just creating the content itself. I intend to showcase my skills, projects, and case studies like this on the website. Skills would be categorized under Web, Apps, Games, General, and Embedded, and could be a Language (like JavaScript), a Framework (like Svelte), or a Tool (like Zed or VSCode). Projects are related to the skills required for them, and a project could involve one or more case studies. The biggest benefit is that now I can spend most of the time on the content, run the static generator, and the portfolio website would be updated!

A 2D puzzle game developed as the semester project for BA1. My focus here was on the light and color based puzzle mechanics of the game, implementing how lights of different colors combined to create a new light, and platforms that would reveal themselves when lights of matching colors would shine on them.

As a student assistant at Cologne Game Lab, I was involved in their part of the Gen-AIvatar research project in cooperation with Uni Bonn and MindPort GmbH. My tasks involved extracting their IMPECT Feedback system server's frontend, establishing a WebSocket based Flask backend, as well as collaborating on a Unity VR client as a use case for animation and emotion feedbacks.

A rhythm-based 2D co-op shooter game developed as the semester project for BA3. My part in this project was setting up an edge-gravity based character controller, and creating weapons which synced bullets with different parts of the music.
My first Portfolio website, created with Angular. At the time, my skill set was more limited, hence the website only catered to showcase the few projects that I've worked on.

A 3D narrative horror-esque game developed as the semester project for BA2. My main contributions to the project were creating shaders for highlighting objects, and implementing raycast proximity for interacting with objects.

As a student assistant at Uni Bonn's faculty of computer science, I helped maintain and polish the website of the department of Visual Computing. This involved porting the vanilla koa-js backend to a headless strapi cms instance, refactoring the frontend vue components and query stores, and writing a go lang script to migrate the data from the old backend to strapi.

Introduction

As a student assistant employed by the Department of Visual Computing in the Institute of Informatics at the University of Bonn, I was tasked with the maintenance and polish of their website. Their backend ran on vanilla KoaJS and MariaDB, and their frontend was rendered with Vue using hand written components. There were a lot of things to improve upon, and I was given a lot of flexibility in choosing which ones to work on first.

Familiarizing myself with the code base

To understand the code better, my first task was to port the way in which the frontend managed state, from Vuex 4 to Pinia (which is essentially Vuex 5). Almost every table in the backend database had an associated store, which meant carefully merging all the different mutations and actions into a single actions object, and making them available along with the state variables through the mapping functions provided by Pinia. At this point, the frontend codebase still used the Options API, and the different Pinia stores also started off with the same API for clarity. Later on, the entire codebase would be ported to the Composition API, along with the stores. Working through all of this provided a good mental model of how data flowed from the backend to the frontend, and was rendered through the different components.

Organizing the UI

After all the stores were ported, my next task was to replace the handwritten UI components with alternatives from a premade UI library, for which we chose PrimeVue. PrimeTek is a Turkish company which creates and maintains an open-source library of UI components for JavaServer Faces, React, Angular, and Vue. With the concern that there could come a time where we might have to shift our entire codebase to another frontend framework, we decided on this library to make our work easier if that happened. Instead of using PrimeVue's own styles however, we mixed it with TailwindCSS to preserve the design guidelines by Uni Bonn.

In the process, we ended up with a clean hierarchy of UI component folders that catered to the different use cases for the website, alongside an App folder for common components. Naturally, the header and footer lived in their own components, but subcomponents were also needed for their different parts, such as the language switcher, the Login Dialog, and the Menu inside the Navbar. Individual Card components were made for different content types. Tables were listed with DataTables and DataViews, with some lists rendered with Accordions. The end result was a very similar looking, but more polished and maintainable version of the website.

Next Steps

Since the backend was created manually, there were a lot of bugs and loopholes resulting in stale data and errors when submitting forms. Therefore, we decided to switch the entire backend itself to a headless cms, and chose Strapi CMS for its popularity. This would mean creating a Strapi instance, setting up all the different content types, and remapping the data fetching to this new backend via the REST API. Such a huge switch took over a year to fully implement without any gaps, and therefore deserves its own part of the case study covering all the different aspects. You can read the next part of this case study here.

Introduction

As a student assistant employed by the Department of Visual Computing in the Institute of Informatics at the University of Bonn, I was tasked with the maintenance and polish of their website. In a few months, I helped port the state management to Pinia, and replace vanilla Vue components with PrimeVue, and by this time I was very familiar with the codebase. With that, our next challenge was to implement a headless CMS so that the backend troubles would be minimized.

How a Headless CMS works

Unlike traditional content-management systems like Wordpress, Drupal, and Joomla, which provide a way to create the frontend along with some styles; headless content-management-systems only provide the backend handling. The user creates all the content, but the frontend, or "head" so to say, is managed elsewhere. This way, we wouldn't have to worry about querying the database ourselves for every single operation. On top of this, Strapi provides a plugin for internationalization, where the same content can be queried in different languages, whereas our current backend used duplicated columns for English and German. For us, this means creating the content types and relations that resembled our current backend as much as possible, then swapping the API calls in our frontend to this new backend, and finally remapping the UI to match the new content schema. Alongside this, we also needed a mechanism to transfer all the data from the old backend to this new one, all the while preserving the data while matching it to the new content fields.

Creating the Strapi Instance

Our old backend had 20 tables, a handful of which were pivot tables, and one for sessions. However for the content types in Strapi, we had to create 40. A few of these were because some new content types were added in between switching to the new backend. A quarter of them were geared towards handling our own sessions, users, and permissions. A bunch of content in the old backend also had a type associated with it, which was a single string column. For the new backend, we extracted them into their own types with a relation.

Extra Customizations

Since authentication on the website was split between Local users and LDAP users, we needed to implement our own User Management system instead of the Users and Permissions plugin provided by Strapi. Alongside this, the old backend provided a mechanism where certain course materials could only be accessed by a dedicated local user, therefore we iterated on this to create a full Role-Based-Access-Control system, where individual entries or entire collections could be restricted to certain users or roles. We also created a content type for routes and middleware, so that the frontend can create the entire route tree from the backend, along with the frontend middleware that would run for each route.

Porting Data to the New Backend

For a seamless transition to the new backend, the data that was in the old backend needed to be made available in Strapi. This meant querying the old backend, splitting entries into two languages based on duplicate columns, and sending it to Strapi via the REST API. In order to accomplish this, we used Golang to create a "porting script". The script would utilize and ORM to query the old backend, and for all of the old content types available, it would create the equivalent content in Strapi and send the data via an HTTP Client.

Querying Strapi from the Frontend

As discussed in the previous part, the state management was ported from Vuex to Pinia by this point. Since data fetching is synchronous, however, this means we were inefficiently querying and storing the data, and should have used a server-state manager instead. So in the process of querying the data from the frontend, we also switched most of the data fetching in the Pinia stores to a new library - Tanstack Vue Query. All the queries to the Strapi REST API were now done through Vue Query, except for authentication calls, which were still managed in Pinia.

Conclusion

Porting a vanilla backend to a headless CMS takes a lot of time depending on the scope. This involves copious amounts of trial-and-error testing, a lot of discussion about what the shape of the content should be, and building it up alongside frontend-mapping and data porting. What started in August of 2024 ended up finishing in March of 2026, when the new backend finally went live. Functionally it was the same, but under the hood there were a lot of changes, distinguishable only by the new UI components developed in between for rendering data and displaying forms. Throughout the process I learned an amazing amount of things. REST APIs, middlewares, and backends finally clicked for me, and the approach we now have towards handling data inspired my next portfolio website project, dubbed Portfolio V3. You can read the case study for creating the website here.
Narrative rhythm game made for a second iteration of the BA2 semester project. I developed the rhythm and lane mechanics, along with transitioning into branching levels based on player gameplay.
